Open Source AI.titleZh: Meta debuts Muse Spark, first AI model under Alexandr Wang - Axios
Open Source AI.content: [Skip to main content](#main-content) [Axios](/) 19 mins ago - [Technology](https://www.axios.com/technology) # Meta debuts Muse Spark, first AI model under Alexandr Wang * [Ina Fried](https://www.axios.com/authors/ina) [Add Axios on Google](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=axios.com) Add Axios as your preferred source to see more of our stories on Google. [Add Axios on Google](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=axios.com) Meta on Wednesday debuted Muse Spark, a homegrown AI model it says significantly narrows the performance gap with models from OpenAI, Anthropic and others. **Why it matters:** The model — code-named Avocado and built over the past nine months by a team led by Alexandr Wang — is a major upgrade over its Llama 4 models, Meta tells. **Driving the news:** Muse Spark will power queries in the Meta AI app and Meta.ai website immediately, with plans to expand across Facebook, Instagram and WhatsApp. * The model accepts voice, text and image inputs, but produces text-only output. * As Axios [first reported](https://www.axios.com/2026/04/06/meta-open-source-ai-models), Meta plans to release a version of Muse Spark under an open-source license. **Zoom in:** The model uses a fast mode for casual queries and several reasoning modes. * A "shopping mode" highlights how Meta hopes to differentiate itself. It combines large language models with data on user interests and behavior. * Over time, the model will also power "features that cite recommendations and content people share across Instagram, Facebook, and Threads," Meta said in a blog post. **Zoom out:** A Meta executive told Axios that Muse Spark doesn't mark a new state of the art, but is competitive with the latest models from leading labs at certain tasks, including multimodal understanding and processing health information. * In other areas, including coding, the company acknowledges that there is a gap between Muse Spark and the models already available. * All flavors of the model are free to use, though Meta may impose rate limits. **Yes, but:** Consumers should be aware that Meta's privacy policy [sets few limits](https://www.axios.com/2024/11/05/meta-ai-user-data-information) on how the company can use any data shared with its AI system. **The big picture:** Meta sees the release of Muse Spark as just one step toward its broader vision of personal superintelligence. * However, Meta's rivals are also leaping forward. Anthropic this week detailed Mythos, a model it says is so powerful that its [initial release](https://www.axios.com/2026/04/07/anthropic-mythos-preview-cybersecurity-risks) is being limited to a handful of tech companies to focus on cybersecurity defense. * OpenAI is also [putting the finishing touches](https://www.axios.com/2026/03/24/openai-discontinue-sora-video-app) on a new model, code-named Spud, that is also believed to represent a significant leap forward. Open Source AI.title: Meta Unveils New AI Model Developed by Costly, New Superintelligence Labs - CNET
Open Source AI.titleZh: Meta Unveils New AI Model Developed by Costly, New Superintelligence Labs - CNET
Open Source AI.content: * News # Meta Unveils New AI Model Developed by Costly, New Superintelligence Labs The model comes from a team of AI leaders and developers poached from other AI companies last year. Steven Musil Night Editor / News Steven Musil is a senior news editor at CNET News. He's been hooked on tech since learning BASIC in the late '70s. When not cleaning up after his daughter and son, Steven can be found pedaling around the San Francisco Bay Area. Before joining CNET in 2000, Steven spent 10 years at various Bay Area newspapers and had a brief stint at MacWeek. Expertise I have more than 35 years' experience in journalism in the heart of Silicon Valley. [See full bio](/profiles/stevenmusil/) Steven Musil 2 min read Meta unveiled its latest artificial intelligence model on Wednesday, its first since assembling a costly team to help the company better compete with its AI rivals. The new model, known as Muse Spark, was created by Meta Superintelligence Labs, a team of expensive AI leaders and developers assembled last year from other AI companies and led by Alexandr Wang, the co-founder and CEO of Scale AI. Wang joined the Meta AI team after Meta agreed to make a $14.3 billion investment in Scale AI. The model, known internally as Avacodo, now powers the Meta AI app and the Meta AI website and will soon power Meta apps WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, Messenger and AI glasses. Meta called the new model "an early data point on our trajectory," adding that it has larger models in development. "This initial model is small and fast by design, yet capable enough to reason through complex questions in science, math, and health," Meta said in its [announcement](https://about.fb.com/news/2026/04/introducing-muse-spark-meta-superintelligence-labs/ "(opens in a new window)"). "It is a powerful foundation, and the next generation is already in development." Meta has been investing increasingly large sums to compete in the ever-evolving AI landscape, creating a "[superintelligence team](https://www.cnet.com/tech/services-and-software/meta-supercharges-ai-projects-in-bid-to-make-llama-everyones-go-to-platform/)" that works toward achieving [artificial general intelligence](https://www.cnet.com/tech/theres-ai-and-then-theres-agi-what-you-need-to-know-to-tell-the-difference/), or AGI. The release of Meta Muse comes after the company's disappointing release of the Llama models last year. Meta has bet big on AI to catch up and keep pace with other AI builders. Google leapfrogged its rivals in November with its [Gemini 3 model](https://www.cnet.com/tech/services-and-software/google-says-new-gemini-3-ai-model-will-better-understand-your-requests/), showcasing its impressive coding and research abilities. OpenAI was quick to follow with [updates to GPT-5](https://www.cnet.com/tech/services-and-software/chatgpt-5-2-arrives-heres-what-it-means-for-everyday-and-work-users/).

Open Source AI.title: Meta’s First AI Model From Its Superintelligence Lab Doesn’t Exactly Spark Joy - Gizmodo
Open Source AI.titleZh: Meta’s First AI Model From Its Superintelligence Lab Doesn’t Exactly Spark Joy - Gizmodo
Open Source AI.content: [Skip to content](#main) [Artificial Intelligence](https://gizmodo.com/tech/artificial-intelligence) # Meta’s First AI Model From Its Superintelligence Lab Doesn’t Exactly Spark Joy Playing catch-up instead of leapfrog. By [AJ Dellinger](https://gizmodo.com/author/ajdellinger1) Reading time 3 minutes [Comments (1)](https://gizmodo.com/metas-first-ai-model-from-its-superintelligence-lab-doesnt-exactly-spark-joy-2000744082#comments) Welcome back to the frontier AI model race, Meta. After [spending billions of dollars](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/01/28/technology/meta-earnings-ai-spending.html) (and churning significant staff) to overhaul its efforts in the space, Mark Zuckerberg’s company finally has a new model to show the world. Dubbed [Spark Muse](https://ai.meta.com/blog/introducing-muse-spark-msl/), the model represents a significant leap over the company’s previous underwhelming efforts and puts it back in the mix with competitors like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google on most major benchmarks for model performance—though it’s not yet challenging for the top spot in most categories. The model, built from scratch by Meta Superintelligence Labs under the leadership of Alexandr Wang, represents something of a fresh start for the company. It claimed the Spark Muse is a “natively multimodal reasoning model with support for tool-use, visual chain of thought, and multi-agent orchestration,” and is built to be integrated directly into other Meta’s products. As such, it’ll reportedly roll out across Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp in the coming weeks. What people can expect, if benchmarks are to be believed (Meta has been [accused of fudging its benchmarks](https://www.ft.com/content/e3c4c2f6-4ea7-4adf-b945-e58495f836c2) in the past and didn’t release a paper along with the model, so maybe take these figures with a heaping helping of salt), is a model that is significantly more capable than prior offerings across basically all categories. By Meta’s own marks, it ranks behind only Google Gemini 3.1 Pro and OpenAI’s GPT-5.4 in multimodal functionality, or the ability to process information across different data streams and formats. It was also competitive in reasoning tests, though it typically fell short of the high marks from Anthropic’s Claude, and the top Gemini and GPT models. Where Spark Muse continues to struggle is with coding and agentic functionality, which allows the model to complete tasks autonomously. It’s functional in those areas in ways that LLaMa 4 wasn’t, but it’s still a ways off from putting any dent in Anthropic’s dominance in the vibe coding sphere. Meta has two things it is hoping will allow its model to stand out. The first is [shopping](https://about.fb.com/news/2026/04/introducing-muse-spark-meta-superintelligence-labs/). Meta claims that Spark Muse is capable of drawing styling inspiration from creators and communities that people follow to offer personalized product recommendations. Affiliate-style sales have been one of the low-hanging fruits for monetization for AI firms, so it’s no surprise Meta is reaching for it. The other area the company is emphasizing is its ability to process health data. Again, it’s a good example of Meta trying to score some easy points, as asking health questions is a [common AI use case](https://www.kff.org/public-opinion/kff-tracking-poll-on-health-information-and-trust-use-of-ai-for-health-information-and-advice/) for consumers. But Meta might have a bit too much baggage when it comes to how the company collects and uses data from its users to get the kind of buy-in it’d need to become anyone’s pocket-bound physician. The release of Spark Muse isn’t exactly earth-shattering—it’s an interesting choice for Meta to drop a perfectly capable model that doesn’t fundamentally shift the landscape while Anthropic is in the middle of yet another “[Our model is so powerful it might destroy the world as we know it](https://gizmodo.com/anthropics-new-model-is-so-scarily-powerf...

Open Source AI.title: Anthropic Project Glasswing to Use AI to Fix Software Bugs With AI - Infosecurity Magazine
Open Source AI.titleZh: Anthropic Project Glasswing to Use AI to Fix Software Bugs With AI - Infosecurity Magazine
Open Source AI.content: [Infosecurity Magazine Home](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/) » [News](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/) » [Anthropic Launches Project Glasswing to Use AI to Find and Fix Critical Software Vulnerabilities](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/anthropic-launch-project-glasswing/) # Anthropic Launches Project Glasswing to Use AI to Find and Fix Critical Software Vulnerabilities [News](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/) ## Written by ### [Danny Palmer](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/profile/danny-palmer/) Deputy Editor, Infosecurity Magazine AI firm Anthropic has launched Project Glasswing, an initiative which uses AI to identify and remediate undiscovered cybersecurity vulnerabilities in critical software. Project Glasswing, named after the glasswing butterfly, is based on Claude Mythos Preview, a powerful, not publicly available, version of [Anthropic’s Large Language Model (LLM)](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news-features/how-safeguard-vibe-coding-security/). The company described the model as the “most capable yet for coding and agentic tasks” and that it can “deeply understand and modify complex software,” allowing Claude Mythos Preview to autonomously find and fix cybersecurity vulnerabilities at scale. Anthropic did not train it specifically for cybersecurity, rather it said the capabilities are the result of its “strong agentic coding and reasoning skills.” Announced publicly on April 7, the capabilities of Claude Mythos Preview have already been tested by Anthrophic’s launch partners for Project Glasswing. These include Amazon Web Services, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorganChase, the Linux Foundation, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Palo Alto Networks. In testing, the model discovered thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities which had not previously been identified. These included: * A 27-year-old vulnerability in OpenBSD, a security-hardened UNIX-like operating system used to run firewalls and other critical infrastructure. The vulnerability allowed an attacker to remotely crash any machine running the operating system just by connecting to it * A 16-year-old vulnerability in FFmpeg, which is commonly used in software to encode and decode video. The vulnerability was discovered in a line of code that automated testing tools had hit five million times without it previously identified * The model autonomously found and chained several vulnerabilities in the Linux kernel, the software which is used to run most of the world’s servers, to allow an attacker to escalate from ordinary user access to complete control of the machine Anthropic said that it had reported the vulnerabilities it discovered to the maintainers of the relevant software. The publicly identified vulnerabilities have already been patched. “Our eventual goal is to enable our users to safely deploy Mythos-class models at scale,” the AI firm said. ## **Open-Source Security Support** As part of Project Glasswing, Anthropic has committed up to $100m in usage credits to over 40 additional organizations that build or maintain critical software infrastructure so they can use the model to scan and secure both first-party and open-source systems. The company will also provide $4m in donations to open-source security organizations to support the work and to develop patches, if necessary. Anthropic said it does not plan to make Claude Mythos Preview publicly available. It is intended for use by cybersecurity defenders and with [appropriate guardrails](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/opinions/clawing-back-security-challenges/) in place. However, threat actors have managed to jailbreak, abuse or even develop their own malicious versions of AI models to [help commit AI-powered cybercrime at scale](https://www.infosecurityeurope.com/en-gb/blog/threat-vectors/how-cisos-can-defend-against-the-rise-of-ai-powered-cybercrime.html) and some industry insiders have voiced concern over the potential for attackers to ge...

Open Source AI.title: Anthropic Says Its New AI Model Is So Good at Finding Security Risks, You Can't Use It - cnet.com
Open Source AI.titleZh: Anthropic Says Its New AI Model Is So Good at Finding Security Risks, You Can't Use It - cnet.com
Open Source AI.content: Why You Can Trust CNET Our expert, award-winning staff selects the products we cover and rigorously researches and tests our top picks. If you buy through our links, we may get a commission. [Reviews ethics statement](https://www.cnet.com/home/how-we-test-everything/) * [ai atlas](/ai-atlas/) * [Tech](/tech/) * Services & Software * [AI](/tech/services-and-software/ai/) # Anthropic Says Its New AI Model Is So Good at Finding Security Risks, You Can't Use It With its new Claude Mythos Preview model, the company is pulling together tech giants for a new cybersecurity consortium, Project Glasswing. Omar Gallaga [See full bio](/profiles/ogallaga/) [Omar Gallaga](/profiles/ogallaga/) 3 min read AI developer Anthropic says its newest [Claude](https://www.cnet.com/tech/services-and-software/anthropics-claude-what-you-need-to-know-about-this-ai-tool/) artificial intelligence model is so good at finding cybersecurity vulnerabilities that it's not releasable to the public. The company is instead providing the tool to big tech infrastructure providers so they can patch the flaws it finds. In late March, [word began to leak](https://fortune.com/2026/03/26/anthropic-says-testing-mythos-powerful-new-ai-model-after-data-leak-reveals-its-existence-step-change-in-capabilities/ "(opens in a new window)") that Anthropic's latest AI model, [dubbed Claude Mythos](https://www-cdn.anthropic.com/8b8380204f74670be75e81c820ca8dda846ab289.pdf "(opens in a new window)") (PDF), was going to be a leap forward for the company's AI technology. Now, the company has previewed its capabilities and warned that Mythos represents a major cybersecurity threat, as its [capabilities represent a leap forward](https://red.anthropic.com/2026/mythos-preview/ "(opens in a new window)") in finding and exploiting online security vulnerabilities. "AI models have reached a level of coding capability where they can surpass all but the most skilled humans at finding and exploiting software vulnerabilities," the company [said in a blog post](https://www.anthropic.com/glasswing "(opens in a new window)") Tuesday. Anthropic said Mythos Preview, which has not been released to the public, has already found what it says are thousands of severe security vulnerabilities "in every major operating system and web browser." Asked for comment, a representative for Anthropic directed CNET to the company's blog post. To address the cybersecurity risks, Anthropic said it's launching a consortium [called Project Glasswing](https://www.anthropic.com/glasswing "(opens in a new window)") that includes Apple, Amazon Web Services, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorgan Chase, the Linux Foundation, Microsoft, Nvidia and Palo Alto Networks. Anthropic said those organizations and more than 40 others will have access to Mythos in order to start the work of shoring up defenses against AI attacks and exploits. It's committing $100 million in usage credits for Mythos and $4 million in donations to open-source security organizations. "The dangers of getting this wrong are obvious, but if we get it right, there is a real opportunity to create a fundamentally more secure internet and world than we had before the advent of AI-powered cyber capabilities," Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei [posted on X](https://x.com/DarioAmodei/status/2041580341794615631 "(opens in a new window)"). In a [video posted to YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=INGOC6-LLv0 "(opens in a new window)") about Project Glasswing, leaders from companies including Microsoft, the Linux Foundation and Anthropic discussed the damage that software vulnerabilities can cause. Large cloud computing companies have already been working with the new model to find vulnerabilities. "What we have found has been illuminating," Anthony Grieco, chief security and trust officer at Cisco, [wrote in a blog post](https://blogs.cisco.com/news/rising-to-the-era-of-ai-powered-cyber-defense "(opens in a new window)"). "Now the real work begins. AI-powered ana...

Infra & Chips.title: AI Data Center Provider Firmus Raises $505 Million Led by Coatue at $5.5B Valuation with Nvidia Involvement - MLQ.ai
Infra & Chips.titleZh: AI Data Center Provider Firmus Raises $505 Million Led by Coatue at $5.5B Valuation with Nvidia Involvement - MLQ.ai
Infra & Chips.content: [AI](/news/tag/ai/) [Tech](/news/tag/tech/) # AI Data Center Provider Firmus Raises $505 Million Led by Coatue at $5.5B Valuation with Nvidia Involvement April 8, 2026 at 9:38 AM • by MLQ Agent #### Key Points * Firmus secured $505 million in Series G funding led by Coatue with participation from NVIDIA[1] * The investment values the AI infrastructure company at $5.5 billion post-money[1] * Funding will accelerate deployment of AI factories across Asia-Pacific and support Project Southgate in Australia[1] * Firmus has raised $1.35 billion in total equity over the past six months[1] * The company previously secured a $10 billion debt financing facility from Blackstone in February[1] Firmus, an Asia-based AI infrastructure company, announced it expects to secure $505 million in strategic equity investment led by Coatue with participation from NVIDIA[1]. The funding round values the company at $5.5 billion and brings total equity raised over the past six months to $1.35 billion[1]. ## Investment Details and Funding Sources The $505 million Series G investment is led by Coatue, a global investor in AI and technology infrastructure, with NVIDIA participating subject to closing conditions[1]. This round follows Firmus' February announcement of a $10 billion debt financing facility led by Blackstone and supported by Coatue[1]. The combination of equity and debt funding positions Firmus as a major player in AI infrastructure development. ## Project Southgate and AI Factory Deployment Firmus' flagship Project Southgate aims to establish a national network of energy-efficient AI factories across Australia[1]. The funding will support rapid deployment of the company's AI infrastructure platform based on the NVIDIA Vera Rubin DSX reference design across the Asia-Pacific region[1]. Firmus delivers its AI Factory platform through a vertically integrated supply chain based in Australia, enabling localized control of infrastructure development[1]. ## Regional AI Infrastructure Expansion The investment targets scaling AI factories across Asia-Pacific to meet growing demand for specialized AI computing facilities[2]. Firmus plans to accelerate AI infrastructure deployment across the region while preparing for a 2026 Australian Securities Exchange listing[2]. This expansion reflects increasing recognition of the Asia-Pacific region as a critical hub for AI infrastructure investment and deployment. ## Valuation Milestone and Company Growth The $5.5 billion valuation reflects significant investor confidence in Firmus' technology platform and market opportunity[1]. The company's rapid capital raises over six months demonstrate strong demand for AI infrastructure solutions and confidence from tier-one technology investors including NVIDIA and major financial backers like Coatue[1]. ## Infrastructure Investment Strategy Firmus' funding success underscores the intense competition for AI infrastructure assets as global technology companies race to build computing capacity for next-generation AI workloads. NVIDIA's participation in the round signals strategic importance—the company is not merely a technology supplier but an active investor in the data center platforms that will run its chips, ensuring compatibility and optimization for future generations of GPU hardware. The $1.35 billion raised in six months reflects market recognition that specialized AI infrastructure tailored for specific workloads commands significant capital deployment.The vertically integrated supply chain based in Australia represents a deliberate differentiation strategy. By controlling manufacturing and deployment locally, Firmus reduces supply chain risk and dependency on third parties while positioning itself for potential export opportunities across Asia-Pacific. This approach contrasts with competitors relying on external suppliers and suggests Firmus is betting on long-term sustainability through vertical control rather than rapid outsourced scaling.The combination of $10...

Infra & Chips.title: AT&T maps its AI-grid edge game with Nvidia, Cisco, Microsoft - RCR Wireless News
Infra & Chips.titleZh: AT&T maps its AI-grid edge game with Nvidia, Cisco, Microsoft - RCR Wireless News
Infra & Chips.content: Wednesday, April 8, 2026 Copyright 2021 - All Right Reserved [AI Infrastructure](https://www.rcrwireless.com/category/ai-infrastructure)[Internet of Things (IoT)](https://www.rcrwireless.com/category/internet-of-things)[IoT](https://www.rcrwireless.com/category/internet-of-things-4)[Network Infrastructure](https://www.rcrwireless.com/category/network-infrastructure)[Private 5G](https://www.rcrwireless.com/category/private-5g)[Private Networks](https://www.rcrwireless.com/category/private-networks) # AT&T maps its AI-grid edge game with Nvidia, Cisco, Microsoft by [James Blackman](https://www.rcrwireless.com/author/jblackman) written by [James Blackman](https://www.rcrwireless.com/author/jblackman) *1* *AT&T has clarified its emerging AI “grid” and IoT strategy, combining regional inference, cloud platforms, and private 5G to target enterprise use cases while testing where edge AI delivers most value.* In sum – what to know: **Regional edge** – AT&T is using Nvidia GPUs at six regional Cisco data centers in the US for inference workloads, initially attached to security cameras; the service will expand internationally. **Cloud platform** – AT&T is offering a Microsoft Azure-based platform for manufacturers to share and analyze machine data across sites, using generative AI for diagnostics and optimization. **Private network** – AT&T has twin private 5G offerings, big and small, with unnamed vendor partners, and contracts with municipalities and car makers; the tech is strategically “important”, it says. Helpfully, AT&T has put its recent ‘AI grid’ and AI factory announcements, from GTC and MWC in March, into some kind of context for us, and also spoken quite clearly (not exactly freely, but candidly, and for the first time) about its business with private networks. *Where to start?* Let’s take them in (reverse-chronological) order, and summarize: AT&T is using Nvidia GPUs at six regional Cisco data centers in the US for inference workloads, initially attached to security cameras, as part of a managed IoT offer for enterprises ([GTC, March 19](https://www.rcrwireless.com/20260319/ai-ml/att-cisco-nvidia-ai)); it has a new Microsoft-based edge-to-cloud IoT platform for multinational manufacturers to share machine data between plants ([MWC, March 5](https://www.rcrwireless.com/20260305/internet-of-things/att-builds-out-connected-ai-strategy-for-industrial-edge)); and it is busy selling two private 5G systems, one big and one small, to US-based enterprises of all sizes. These projects might be considered among a rush of related releases from the firm, geared variously to put AI to work in the network and on the network: a new preview of [AWS Interconnect](https://www.rcrwireless.com/20260303/network-infrastructure/att-combines-with-aws-in-metro-ericsson-in-ran-azure-at-edge) with AWS, embedding fiber and 5G FWA directly into AWS workflows to support latency-sensitive AI; a recent demo with Ericsson of ‘AI-native’ link adaptation on an Intel-based cloud RAN stack, claiming efficiency gains of up to 20 percent over legacy systems; a broad fiber target to reach over [40 million](https://www.rcrwireless.com/20260129/carriers/att-beats-targets) US customer locations by the end of 2026, boosted by its Gigapower FFTH joint-venture with Blackrock and acquisition of Lumen’s consumer business. There has been lots more from AT&T lately, as well, but its discussion with *RCR* is about IoT and AI, mostly, as sold by its AT&T Business function. Cameron Coursey, vice president of connected solutions at AT&T, is on the end of the line, and says more besides. “We are working at providing AI inference at the switch locations for 4G, and eventually 5G – in the data centers. This is not about putting [AI] in cell sites – which some other operators are looking at. I’m also not looking at AI for the RAN or AI in the network itself. This is for customers in the enterprise segment in IoT and how we can provide a capability there,” he explains. There is a su...

By[Keithen Drury](/author/20439/) – Apr 8, 2026 at 2:15AM EST ## Key Points * Nvidia believes there will be a huge uptick in data center spending. * Nvidia could be worth more than several tech giants combined by 2030. Finding a stock that will be worth more than **Microsoft** ([MSFT](/quote/nasdaq/msft/) 0.16%), **Alphabet** ([GOOG](/quote/nasdaq/goog/) +2.11%) ([GOOGL](/quote/nasdaq/googl/) +1.69%), and **Palantir** ([PLTR](/quote/nasdaq/pltr/) +1.45%) combined seems like a far-fetched notion. Right now, these three stocks are worth $6.65 trillion combined. By 2030, this trio could be worth as much as $10 trillion after growing to expand into the new realm of artificial intelligence (AI). However, I think there's one company that could achieve that goal, and it's the one making all of this AI technology possible: **Nvidia** ([NVDA](/quote/nasdaq/nvda/) +0.07%). Nvidia is already a $4.2 trillion company, but I think it could be far larger by the end of 2030. Image source: Getty Images. ## Nvidia projects huge growth through 2030 Nvidia makes graphics processing units (GPUs) that are suited for a wide range of accelerated computing tasks. Originally, they were developed for gaming graphics, then saw increased usage in engineering simulations, drug discovery, cryptocurrency mining, and ultimately their biggest use case yet: AI. GPUs thrive with any workload that requires massive computing power, and with AI being the biggest computing workload to date, Nvidia is perfectly positioned to take advantage as a company. Nvidia has delivered incredible growth rates since the AI race began in 2023. [NVDA Revenue (Quarterly YoY Growth)](https://ycharts.com/companies/NVDA/revenues_growth) data by [YCharts](https://ycharts.com) While its growth rates started to dip in 2025, they are reaccelerating now. For the first quarter, analysts project 79% growth. In Q2, they expect 85% growth. AI demand clearly isn't slowing, and Nvidia is capitalizing on that. But what does the future hold? Nvidia believes that by 2030, [data center capital expenditures](https://www.fool.com/research/ai-companies-spending-on-data-centers/) will reach $3 trillion to $4 trillion annually. That includes every country in the world, so this projection isn't as far-fetched as it seems, as the big four AI hyperscalers are spending around $650 billion this year. In 2025, Nvidia estimated that this spending was about $600 billion. Last year, the company generated $216 billion in revenue -- about a 36% spending capture rate. ## [NASDAQ: NVDA](/quote/nasdaq/nvda/) Nvidia Today's Change (0.07%) $0.13 Current Price $177.77 ### Key Data Points Market Cap $4.3T Day's Range $173.66- $177.87 52wk Range $95.04- $212.19 Volume 43K Av...

Infra & Chips.title: Firefly Aerospace Enables On-Orbit Processing for Moon Imaging Service with NVIDIA Jetson - Firefly Aerospace
Infra & Chips.titleZh: Firefly Aerospace Enables On-Orbit Processing for Moon Imaging Service with NVIDIA Jetson - Firefly Aerospace
Infra & Chips.content: [Skip to content](#content) [< Back to News](https://fireflyspace.com/news/) # **Firefly Aerospace Enables On-Orbit Processing for Moon Imaging Service with NVIDIA Jetson** *Firefly’s AI software will run on NVIDIA Jetson module onboard Elytra spacecraft in lunar orbit to enable faster data-driven insights from the Moon* **CEDAR PARK, Texas, April 8, 2026** – [Firefly Aerospace](https://fireflyspace.com/) (Nasdaq: FLY), a market leading space and defense technology company, today announced a collaboration with NVIDIA to enable rapid on-orbit processing in lunar orbit for Firefly’s [Ocula](https://fireflyspace.com/ocula/) Moon imaging service. As part of the collaboration, an [NVIDIA Jetson](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/autonomous-machines/embedded-systems/) module was embedded on [high-resolution Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory telescopes](https://fireflyspace.com/news/firefly-aerospace-announces-new-lunar-imaging-service-on-its-elytra-spacecraft/) and delivered to Firefly’s spacecraft facility ahead of integration on its Elytra orbital vehicle. “Ocula is set to be the first commercial lunar imaging and mapping service available on the market, and it’s coming at a critical time when other government-owned satellites in lunar orbit are nearing end of life,” said Jason Kim, CEO of Firefly Aerospace. “Now through our collaboration with NVIDIA, Ocula will be powered by the world’s leading edge AI processor. This capability allows us to layer on our SciTec AI software as the ‘brains’ that give customers real-time data driven insights from the Moon.” Firefly’s Ocula service will be activated onboard Elytra as part of Firefly’s second mission to the Moon, [Blue Ghost Mission 2](https://fireflyspace.com/missions/blue-ghost-mission-2/), targeted to launch no earlier than late 2026. Elytra will first serve as a transfer vehicle and long-haul communications relay for Firefly’s Blue Ghost lander. Elytra will then remain operational in lunar orbit for approximately five years, capturing continuous imagery for the Ocula service in support of advanced lunar surface mapping, mineral detection, and reconnaissance. Firefly’s Ocula data will be rapidly processed onboard Elytra and autonomously transmitted back to Earth utilizing the NVIDIA Jetson module combined with Firefly’s AI software enabled by its [SciTec subsidiary](https://scitec.com/). This allows Firefly to mitigate downlink constraints from the Moon by processing data on orbit before it is transmitted to Earth as real-time, actionable insights for government and commercial customers. “Modern space missions generate massive volumes of data that require immediate processing to overcome the latency and bandwidth constraints of deep-space communications,” said Deepu Talla, vice president of Robotics and Edge AI at NVIDIA. “Integrating the NVIDIA Jetson platform into Firefly’s Elytra spacecraft enables autonomous, on-orbit AI processing that transforms raw lunar imagery into actionable insights in real time.” Firefly’s AI-powered software will further enable advanced space domain awareness in lunar orbit. These AI algorithms and data fusion technologies, already proven in critical [national security missions](https://scitec.com/space-force-operationally-accepts-scitecs-revolutionary-missile-warning-system/) in Earth orbit, will enable Elytra to leverage multiple data feeds onboard to more accurately track maneuvering objects and provide timely situational awareness of space operations occurring in the cislunar domain. Following Blue Ghost Mission 2, Firefly is on contract to deploy two additional Elytra vehicles to lunar orbit as part of [Blue Ghost Mission 3](https://fireflyspace.com/missions/blue-ghost-mission-3/) and [Mission 4](https://fireflyspace.com/missions/blue-ghost-mission-4/), enabling faster revisit times for space domain awareness, lunar surface mapping, and resource detection. ## Latest News ### **Firefly Aerospace Supports U.S. Space Force VICTUS DIEM Exercise** [Rea...

As part of the collaboration, an [NVIDIA Jetson](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/autonomous-machines/embedded-systems/) module was embedded on [high-resolution Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory telescopes](https://fireflyspace.com/news/firefly-aerospace-announces-new-lunar-imaging-service-on-its-elytra-spacecraft/) and delivered to Firefly’s spacecraft facility ahead of integration on its Elytra orbital vehicle. “Ocula is set to be the first commercial lunar imaging and mapping service available on the market, and it’s coming at a critical time when other government-owned satellites in lunar orbit are nearing end of life,” said Jason Kim, CEO of Firefly Aerospace. “Now through our collaboration with NVIDIA, Ocula will be powered by the world’s leading edge AI processor. This capability allows us to layer on our SciTec AI software as the ‘brains’ that give customers real-time data driven insights from the Moon.” Firefly’s Ocula service will be activated onboard Elytra as part of Firefly’s second mission to the Moon, [Blue Ghost Mission 2](https://fireflyspace.com/missions/blue-ghost-mission-2/), targeted to launch no earlier than late 2026. Elytra will first serve as a transfer vehicle and long-haul communications relay for Firefly’s Blue Ghost lander. Elytra will then remain operational in lunar orbit for approximately five years, capturing continuous imagery for the Ocula service in support of advanced lunar surface mapping, mineral detection, and reconnaissance. Firefly’s Ocula data will be rapidly processed onboard Elytra and autonomously transmitted back to Earth utilizing the NVIDIA Jetson module combined with Firefly’s AI software enabled by its [SciTec subsidiary](https://scitec.com/). This allows Firefly to mitigate downlink constraints from the Moon by processing data on orbit before it is transmitted to Earth as real-time, actionable insights for government and commercial customers. “Modern space missions generate massive volumes of data that require immediate processing to overcome the latency and bandwidth constraints of deep-space communications,” said Deepu Talla, vice president of Robotics and Edge AI at NVIDIA. “Integrating the NVIDIA Jetson platform into Firefly’s Elytra spacecraft enables autonomous, on-orbit AI processing that transforms raw lunar imagery into actionable insights in real time.” Firefly’s AI-powered software will further enable advanced space domain awareness in lunar orbit. These AI algorithms and data fusion technologies, already proven in critical [national security missions](https://scitec.com/space-force-operationally-accepts-scitecs-revolutionary-missile-warning-system/) in Earth orbit, will enable Elytra to leverage multiple data feeds onboard to more accurately track maneuvering objects and provide timely situational awareness of space operations occurring in the cislunar domain. Following Blue Ghost Mission 2, Firefly is on contract to deploy two additional Elytra vehicles to lunar orbit as part of [Blue Ghost Mission 3](https://fireflyspace.com/missions/blue-ghost-mission-3/) and [Mission 4](https://fireflyspace.com/missions/blue-ghost-mission-4/), enabling faster revisit times for space domain awareness, lunar surface mapping, and resource detection. ## Latest News ### **Firefly Aerospace Supports U.S. Space Force VICTUS DIEM Exercise** [Rea...

Infra & Chips.title: Imperial County OKs Lot Merger for Data Center - Calexico Chronicle
Infra & Chips.titleZh: Imperial County OKs Lot Merger for Data Center - Calexico Chronicle
Infra & Chips.content: Supervisor Martha Cardenas-Singh on the second floor of the Imperial County Administration Building looks outside to the crowd of people denied entry to the county building and calls for overflow rooms to be opened for the crowd on Tuesday, April 7th. YAMIR AGUILAR PHOTO [Home](https://calexicochronicle.com) [Featured Stories](https://calexicochronicle.com/category/featured-stories/) In [Featured Stories](https://calexicochronicle.com/category/featured-stories/)[Regional News](https://calexicochronicle.com/category/regional-news/) # Imperial County OKs Lot Merger, Easing Development for Data Center [YAMIR AGUILAR](https://calexicochronicle.com/author/yamir-aguilar/ "Posts by YAMIR AGUILAR")on Share E**L CENTRO** —  Tensions ran high inside a strict Imperial County Board of Supervisors meeting on Tuesday, April 7, in which the board approved a lot merger that would facilitate the construction of the proposed hyperscale AI data center from developer Imperial Valley Computer Manufacturing. The county board approved the lot merger in a 4-1 vote, with Martha Cardenas Singh voting no. The lot merger combines several parcels of land located in the southwest corner of Aten Road and Clark Road, and parts of Leimgruber Road. The board voted to vacate an approximately 1,429-foot section of Leimgruber Road in preparation for the lot merger. While the vote does mean a step toward the data center project, there are still more hurdles that Imperial Valley Computer Manufacturing would need to overcome in order to begin its data center. Planning and Development Services Director Jim Minnick made it clear that the developer would need to reach water and electrical agreements with the Imperial Irrigation District before it is able to utilize the space for the data center. Voices in support and in opposition for the data center ran loud in the low-tolerance meeting, but the majority of the people in the meeting room were members of the Laborers’ International Union of America, who adorned their labor union attire in support of the data center. Other members in the crowd who wore safety vests with no union markings were volunteers who heard of the meeting either online or at Imperial Valley College. “Every person behind me in orange represents a family. Whether they’re a brother, a sister, a mom, a dad, we have the same responsibility as everybody else in this community to provide for our family,” said Jose Garcia, life-long member of LIUNA, in his public comment. The majority of the public comments were in opposition to the data center, with recurring worries of the environmental impact of the project. “You as labor members are looking for two years of work. I, however, I’m looking out for the rest of my life and the life of my family. Not all money is good money,” said Carolina Paez in between tears. Paez is a Victoria Ranch resident, one of the neighborhoods located in Imperial, which is in close proximity to where the data center is planned to be built. The space for the meeting was severely limited, with more than 70 people waiting outside because they were not allowed inside the building. No overflow rooms were set up for the expected large turnout until halfway through the meeting, when Supervisor Martha Cardenas Singh, concerned for the people outside in the heat, stepped away from the meeting until overflow rooms were accommodated. “I will not be quiet, I will not, this is unacceptable,” said Cardenas Singh. In total, three individuals opposing the data center project were removed from the meeting at different times at the request of County Executive Officer Kathleen Lang. In the case of Ismael Arvizu, who gave his public comment against the lot merger, he was forcibly removed by a group of armed sheriff’s deputies. Arvizu was one of the many people clapping after a public comment, and was heard saying, “Vote no.” Following this, he was pushed out of the room and surrounded by deputies while he was arrested. Infra & Chips.content: [Skip to content](#main) Covering the business and politics of space [Sign In](#) [Home](https://spacenews.com/) / **April 30: Opportunities for On-Orbit Computing** Posted in[News](https://spacenews.com/section/news-archive/) # April 30: Opportunities for On-Orbit Computing Join us in Washington, D.C., for the next event in our orbital data centers series by [SpaceNews Editor](https://spacenews.com/author/spacenews-editor/) * [Share on X (Opens in new window) X](https://spacenews.com/opportunities-for-on-orbit-computing-event/?share=x&nb=1) * [Share on LinkedIn (Opens in new window) LinkedIn](https://spacenews.com/opportunities-for-on-orbit-computing-event/?share=linkedin&nb=1) * [Share on Facebook (Opens in new window) Facebook](https://spacenews.com/opportunities-for-on-orbit-computing-event/?share=facebook&nb=1) * [Share on Reddit (Opens in new window) Reddit](https://spacenews.com/opportunities-for-on-orbit-computing-event/?share=reddit&nb=1) * [Email a link to a friend (Opens in new window) Email](mailto:?subject=%5BShared%20Post%5D%20April%2030%3A%20Opportunities%20for%20On-Orbit%20Computing&body=https%3A%2F%2Fspacenews.com%2Fopportunities-for-on-orbit-computing-event%2F&share=email&nb=1) [Request to Attend](#h-request-to-attend) Orbital data centers have quickly moved from hypothetical to seemingly inevitable, and opportunities and challenges are coming into focus fast. As players raise millions to drive the race to establish off-planet computing, join SpaceNews on April 30 in Washington, D.C., for an afternoon of conversations with industry and government leaders on building, powering and managing data infrastructure in space. We’ll explore into the near- and long-term potential fueling the buzz and investment in on-orbit computing, assess how solar and nuclear power could solve a key hurdle and weigh how the proliferation of AI informs the calculus of what computing stays on Earth and what begins to be handled in space. *A networking reception will follow our program. The event will not be live-streamed, but a post-event report featuring takeaways will be published in May and select conversations may be featured on upcoming episodes of our Space Minds podcast.* ### Speakers * Marc Berte, Founder and CEO, Overview Energy * Kelley Litzner, Director of Commercial Programs, The Aerospace Corporation * Mike Gruss, Chief Content and Strategy Officer, SpaceNews * *More speakers to be added* [Request to Attend](#h-request-to-attend) --- *In-person event* **Opportunities for On-Orbit Computing** **Part of SpaceNews’ Orbital Data Centers Series** Thursday, April 30 | 3 p.m. to 7 p.m. ET Washington, D.C. --- ### Request to Attend Space for this in-person event is limited. Please complete this interest form and our team will follow up with more details to confirm if your attendance is approved. --- SPONSORED BY Star Catcher is pioneering the first orbital energy grid, the Star Catcher Network, to eliminate power constraints for satellites and other spacecraft. Leveraging proven optical power beaming technologies, the grid delivers concentrated solar energy to client satellites, refined into wavelengths optimized for existing satellite solar panels. With no retrofit or custom receiver required, client satellites can generate up to 10x more power than onboard systems alone, enabling more power-hungry payloads and complex missions, all while reducing upfront costs and complexities associated with larger satellite buses and solar arrays. Learn more at [star-catcher.com](http://star-catcher.com/). ### *Related* ## Sign in [Continue](#) [Set a password (optional)](#) ## Gift this article
